annotations.text: Severe hypertension can cause mental-status changes with psychosis, but I would expect encephalopathy with confusion and other end-organ involvement to be present.

Delirium, which is accompanied by psychosis in about 50% of cases, is unlikely in this case, because the patient’s psychiatric symptoms were neither acute nor characterized by a waxing and waning course.
The absence of clinically significant mood symptoms argues against a diagnosis of psychotic mood disorders. In contrast, this patient has the characteristic symptoms of schizophrenia.

Although schizophrenia is a diagnosis of exclusion, the diagnosis is nevertheless based on observable clinical features and history. On the basis of the information available at the time of this patient’s presentation, the probable diagnosis is schizophrenia that is most likely associated with cognitive impairment and is perhaps exacerbated by her previous traumatic brain injury.
In constructing a differential diagnosis for possible causes of psychosis in this patient, it is helpful to distinguish between primary and secondary psychosis.
The patient’s lack of insight into the nature of her illness is common in schizophrenia and often points toward higher-order cognitive problems.
This patient had elongated toenails that wrapped underneath her toes, suggesting a chronic course of psychiatric illness with limited insight.
her previous traumatic brain injury is relevant because it could suggest that her presentation includes apathy.
The initial goal is to identify potential causes of the patient’s mental state without making a premature diagnosis.
I devoted most of our encounters to simply listening to her talk in an effort to build rapport and understand her narrative.
fatigue. Her descriptions of these ailments were often disorganized and interspersed with delusional thought content and perceptual disturbances.
